blocks.text: and said to them, “Go into the village ahead of you, and immediately as you enter it, you will find tied there a colt that has never been ridden; untie it and bring it.
2 : , associated with God's designated ruler (Zech 9.9; cf. Mt 21.5; Jn 12.15).
If anyone says to you, ‘Why are you doing this?’ just say this, ‘The Lord needs it and will send it back here immediately.’”
3 : , an unusual reference to Jesus in Mark (cf. 5.19n.).
They went away and found a colt tied near a door, outside in the street. As they were untying it,
some of the bystanders said to them, “What are you doing, untying the colt?”
They told them what Jesus had said; and they allowed them to take it.
Then they brought the colt to Jesus and threw their cloaks on it; and he sat on it.
Many people spread their cloaks on the road, and others spread leafy branches that they had cut in the fields.
8 : , details associated with royal processions (see 2 Kings 9.13; 1 Macc 13.51; 2 Macc 10.7).
Then those who went ahead and those who followed were shouting, “Hosanna! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord!
9 : , lit., “save now!” The people's hope for deliverance from the Roman occupation is misplaced; their disappointment will, in part, lead to Jesus's death.
Blessed is the coming kingdom of our ancestor David! Hosanna in the highest heaven!”
9–10 : The crowd's acclaim, based on Ps 118.25–26, indicates they view him as a coming king.
10 : , a phrase that further underscores the crowd's political hopes for Jesus's “kingship”; see 10.47–48n. Cf. Mt 21.9; Lk 1.32–33.
Then he entered Jerusalem and went into the temple; and when he had looked around at everything, as it was already late, he went out to Bethany with the twelve.
11 : Jesus's visit to the impressive Temple structure rebuilt under Herod the Great (37–4 ) apparently goes unnoticed.
On the following day, when they came from Bethany, he was hungry.
Seeing in the distance a fig tree in leaf, he went to see whether perhaps he would find anything on it. When he came to it, he found nothing but leaves, for it was not the season for figs.
13 : , the Greek term “kairos” connotes time that carries a divine purpose, rather than simply chronological time. Cf. 1.15.
He said to it, “May no one ever eat fruit from you again.” And his disciples heard it.
14 : Jesus's curse seems arbitrary—and thus symbolic—since fruit trees’ production depends on the horticultural cycle.

Then they came to Jerusalem. And he entered the temple and began to drive out those who were selling and those who were buying in the temple, and he overturned the tables of the money changers and the seats of those who sold doves;

15 : , a verb often associated in Mark with the casting out of demonic forces; see, e.g., 1.34,39; 3.15,22. , cf. Zech 14.21. Pilgrims exchanged Roman for Temple currency in the Court of the Gentiles. , sacrificial animals (see Lev 1.15; 5.7).
and he would not allow anyone to carry anything through the temple.
He was teaching and saying, “Is it not written, ‘My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations’? But you have made it a den of robbers.”
17 : Jesus cites Isa 56.7 and Jer 7.11 in his prophetic indictment of the Temple economy. , implicates Temple authorities for profiting at the expense of others.
And when the chief priests and the scribes heard it, they kept looking for a way to kill him; for they were afraid of him, because the whole crowd was spellbound by his teaching.
18 : , lit., “destroy him” (see 3.6). The leaders’ malicious intent stems at least partly from Jesus's acclaim among
And when evening came, Jesus and his disciples went out of the city.
In the morning as they passed by, they saw the fig tree withered away to its roots.
Then Peter remembered and said to him, “Rabbi, look! The fig tree that you cursed has withered.”
Jesus answered them, “Have faith in God.
22 : , or “trust,” in God's sovereign power (1.15); see 2.5n.
Truly I tell you, if you say to this mountain, ‘Be taken up and thrown into the sea,’ and if you do not doubt in your heart, but believe that what you say will come to pass, it will be done for you.

23 : , perhaps signaling Mount Zion, linked in Jewish tradition to God's choice of Israel for the renewal of the earth (see, e.g., Isa 2.2–4; 24.23). Roman engineers “moved” mountains to build structures that maintained their occupying power.
So I tell you, whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours.
“Whenever you stand praying, forgive, if you have anything against anyone; so that your Father in heaven may also forgive you your trespasses.”
11.12–25 [26]: Jesus's prophetic indictment of the Temple (Mt 21.12–22; Lk 19.45–48; Jn 2.13–22).
22–25 : Jesus's effective curse on the fig tree displays the connection between “faith in God” and access to divine power.
25 : Cf. Mt 6.5; Lk 18.11,13. Access to divine power depends on right relationships with God and other people.
Again they came to Jerusalem. As he was walking in the temple, the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders came to him
and said, “By what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you this authority to do them?”
28 : , Jesus's authority has been noted from early in the Gospel (see, e.g., 1.22,27; 2.10).
Jesus said to them, “I will ask you one question; answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I do these things.
Did the baptism of John come from heaven, or was it of human origin? Answer me.”
29–30 : In rabbinical fashion, Jesus answers one question with another; cf. 2.25–26; 10.3. , see 1.4–11; 6.14.
They argued with one another, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ he will say, ‘Why then did you not believe him?’
But shall we say, ‘Of human origin’?”—they were afraid of the crowd, for all regarded John as truly a prophet.

32 : , see 11.18n. Since John had stirred hopes for God's coming kingdom, the Jerusalem leaders seem wary about both a possible popular insurgency and the violent response such an uprising would inevitably elicit from Roman officials.
So they answered Jesus, “We do not know.” And Jesus said to them, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things.”

3.19b–35: Jesus at home (Mt 12.22–32; Lk 11.14–23; 12.10). An intertwined intercalation of two passages, linked by the motif of household. For other examples of this technique, see 5.21–43; 11.12–25[26]; 14.1–11.
8.27–15.47: Jesus's messianic mission: sacrifice.
11.1–15.47: Jesus's destiny in Jerusalem.
11.1–13.37: Jesus in Jerusalem, during Passover.
11.27–33: Jerusalem leaders question Jesus's authority (Mt 21.23–27; Lk 20.1–8).
33 : , a safe—and ironic—answer. Cf. 12.24.
